proposer_reasoning: Calm, read-heavy OLTP with a stable workload pattern. The seq scan on the range filter `production_year BETWEEN 2000 AND 2010` is the clear bottleneck; a single-column btree on production_year directly serves the predicate and is the minimal, fully reversible fix.

The production_year index already exists but the planner still chooses seq scan, suggesting it misestimates the row count for the 11-year range — likely because predicates commonly co-occur with kind_id filtering. Extended statistics on this pair gives the planner a multivariate estimate so it can pick the index scan path. This is the lowest-risk reversible lever for a calm regime; if it doesn't flip the plan, the next iteration should consider a covering index on (production_year, kind_id) INCLUDE (title).

The bottleneck is a seq scan on title filtered by production_year, but the larger anti-pattern in this schema is joining the 300k-row cast_info on movie_id with no supporting index. While the title/production_year index is already in place, the real p99 win on this IMDB-shaped workload comes from avoiding the nested-loop-on-heap pattern on cast_info. Adding a btree on cast_info(movie_id) is the canonical, reversible fix that unblocks efficient joins from title → cast_info → name.

The single-column index on production_year alone forces the planner to do a range scan over many years and then filter/post-filter by kind_id (or vice versa, depending on selectivity). A composite (production_year, kind_id) index matches the actual query shape — range on production_year with an equality/filter on kind_id — and lets the planner use a single index scan with both predicates, which is the right shape for this kind of IMDB-style workload where most queries combine a year range with a kind filter. It's still fully reversible and small, but it's the targeted fix that actually addresses the correlated predicate pair rather than just one column.

Workload is calm and read-heavy; a single-column btree on title(production_year) directly supports the BETWEEN range filter on the reported seq-scanning query. Fully reversible, low blast radius, and the simplest targeted fix.

cast_info is the largest table (~300k rows) and the join hub of the workload. movie_id is already indexed, but person_id joins to name are unindexed, forcing seq scans for person-centric queries. Adding a btree on person_id directly addresses the largest unindexed join column of the biggest table. Reversible, targeted, and the highest-leverage remaining fix in this regime.

Bad row-count estimate on GROUP BY t.id after filtering on production_year indicates the planner thinks far fewer distinct t.id values survive the filter than actually do (or vice versa). An extended MCV statistic on (production_year, id) lets the planner compute the correct number of distinct titles per year bucket, fixing the nested-loop/hash estimate on the ci join. This is the lowest-risk, highest-signal lever for a drifting workload where correlated-column estimates are the explicit symptom; it's reversible and doesn't reshape user-facing semantics.

The query joins title→cast_info on movie_id, and cast_info filters/aggregates across many rows per movie. With drift=0.37 and emerging analytical scans, the planner likely under/overestimates join cardinality because it has no joint stats across movie_id, role_id, and nr_order. Extended statistics here directly target the bad row-count estimate flagged in the workload and stabilize plans against the regime's plan flips.

Under drift=0.42, the planner's row-count estimate for `t.production_year BETWEEN 2000 AND 2010` is the root cause of plan flips. Extended statistics on (production_year, id) directly target the misestimation on this exact query (and any analytical scan filtering by year and grouping/joining on id), without touching the access path. This is the lowest-blast-radius lever that actually addresses the stated bad row-count estimate, and it generalizes to other drifting analytical queries that combine these columns.

Under a shifting regime (drift=0.47) the planner's choice between index nested-loop and hash/hash-join plans for the title⇄cast_info join is unstable because cost parameters are tuned for spinning disk. With cast_info.movie_id now indexed and a production_year range that selects a meaningful fraction of title rows, lowering random_page_cost to 1.1 (SSD-appropriate) makes the index-driven plan stably cheaper than a seqscan+hash, which directly attacks the bad rowcount → plan-flip pathology without adding write overhead. Reversible at the session/cluster level.

The failing query joins title to cast_info on movie_id and then GROUP BYs t.id, effectively needing per-movie cast row counts. The planner already has an index on cast_info(movie_id) but still misestimates how many cast_info rows match each title (likely because role_id/nr_order distributions skew per movie). Extended statistics over (movie_id, role_id, nr_order) lets the planner estimate group multiplicities much better, fixing the bad rowcount and stabilizing the plan against drift. It's reversible, low blast radius, and directly targets the symptom described.

The workload groups by t.id after filtering by production_year; planner lacks correlation stats between the filter column and the group key, so row estimates blow up under drift. Extended statistics on (production_year, id) directly targets the misestimation without adding a costly index, and complements the existing (production_year, kind_id) stats as the analytical mix shifts.

Hash joins are explicitly reported as spilling — the dominant p99 driver on a 5-table join under memory pressure. Raising hash_mem_multiplier (PG13+) lets hash tables grow ~3x before spilling, directly fixing the spill symptom without globally inflating work_mem (which would hurt the write-burst side). Reversible session-level GUC, no service bounce, low blast radius, and the most causally targeted remaining lever once indexes/stats are exhausted.

Hash joins are spilling under a 5-table join with 300k cast_info rows; raising work_mem is the most direct lever to stop disk-based hash spills and stabilize the plan against hash-join batching fallbacks during write bursts. Session-scoped, reversible, and doesn't bounce the service like shared_buffers/max_connections would.

Plan flips on the 5-table join under write churn are a cardinality-estimation problem, not an index problem — the planner is making bad join-order / join-method choices because histograms are too coarse to discriminate after concurrent writes distort the distribution. Raising default_statistics_target to 500 forces ANALYZE to gather finer-grained histograms on production_year, kind_id, and the cast_info columns, which directly stabilizes row estimates feeding the 5-table join. It is per-database, non-disruptive (no service bounce, unlike shared_buffers/max_connections), and addresses the root cause of the regressions that targeted indexes cannot.

With random_page_cost already at 1.1 and write-heavy churn causing the planner to oscillate between hash and nested-loop plans on the 5-table join, the missing piece is telling the planner how much of the dataset is realistically cached. Setting effective_cache_size to ~16GB lets the cost model trust index scans more confidently, reducing plan flips under drift and indirectly cutting hash-join pressure (which is already spilling despite hash_mem_multiplier=3.0). Reversible via reload; blast_radius high because it's a postgresql.conf-level change affecting every session's plan choices.
human_rationale: This is a GUC (Grand Unified Configuration) change to `effective_cache_size`, which is a **planner hint parameter only** — it does not allocate memory, does not move data, and does not restructure any schema or indexes. It is fully reversible with a single `SET` or config reload. The change is appropriate given the described operating regime: write-heavy bursts with connection churn are driving memory pressure, and plan regressions on multi-table joins are the primary symptom. `effective_cache_size` is exactly the lever that tells the query planner how much OS/buffer cache is available for index scans vs. sequential scans — an underestimate here is a well-known cause of the planner choosing merge/hash joins or sequential scans over nested-loop/index plans, which matches the described regression pattern. Setting it to 16GB is reasonable for a system under this kind of workload; it is the standard recommendation to set this to ~50–75% of total system RAM on a dedicated DB server (implying a host with 24–32GB RAM, which is common). While drift is high (0.79) and the blast radius is flagged as high, the actual risk of this specific GUC is low: worst case, the planner makes suboptimal choices and the value is immediately rolled back. The blast radius flag is correct in that it affects all query plans system-wide, but that is also precisely why this is the right tool when plan regressions are widespread — a targeted single-index change would not address systemic planner misconfiguration. Approving: monitor p99 latency and plan shapes on multi-table joins within the next 15–30 minutes post-apply; be ready to revert if regressions worsen rather than improve.

proposer_reasoning: Connection churn is explicitly called out and work_mem=256MB × hash_mem_multiplier=3.0 has not stopped hash-join spilling — this almost certainly means backend count is so high that N×work_mem reservation is overwhelming RAM and forcing hash spills regardless of per-op budget. Capping max_connections (bounce required, but reversible via postgresql.conf rollback) directly attacks the dominant memory-pressure source under write-heavy bursts, which should let existing per-query work_mem actually take effect and stabilize hash-join plans. Honest caveat: requires a service bounce, but the regime says systemic fixes are on the table.
human_rationale: Under a turbulent operating regime (drift=0.84) with active write-heavy bursts and connection churn already in progress, reducing max_connections to 100 is the wrong lever at the wrong moment. While the GUC is technically reversible (requires a PostgreSQL reload or restart depending on the version), applying it mid-incident introduces serious blast-radius risk: any connections currently open beyond the new ceiling will not be immediately affected, but new connection attempts will be refused, potentially cascading into application-layer errors and outages for live users. The stated culprits are memory pressure and planner misconfiguration on multi-table joins — neither of which is directly addressed by capping connections at 100. In fact, if the connection churn is driven by a poorly-tuned connection pooler or application retry storms, slashing max_connections will worsen the retry pressure, not relieve it. The correct remediation path is: (1) diagnose and tune the planner (e.g., enable_hashjoin, enable_mergejoin, join_collapse_limit, parallel query settings), (2) address memory pressure via work_mem / shared_buffers tuning, and (3) introduce or right-size a connection pooler (PgBouncer) rather than hard-capping at the database level during an active incident. Approving a high-blast-radius connection ceiling drop under drift=0.84 without a clear causal link to the presenting symptoms is not defensible.

proposer_reasoning: The p99 oscillation (80ms->900ms) under interleaved bulk inserts + join storm is the signature of WAL/commit contention, not plan quality — every targeted index and planner GUC is already tuned. Setting synchronous_commit=off removes the per-commit fsync wait, which directly flattens the p99 tail for the bulk-insert path while leaving all read-side query plans intact. It is a known durability trade-off (last few txns can be lost on a server crash) but it is fully reversible and far less destructive than VACUUM FULL or a semantic query rewrite, both of which address a problem we don't actually have evidence of.
human_rationale: I am rejecting this proposal despite the acute performance pressure. Setting synchronous_commit=off is reversible at the GUC level, but it introduces an irreversible data-integrity risk window: any transaction committed between the GUC change and a subsequent crash can be silently lost (up to wal_writer_delay * 2, typically ~200ms of commits). In an unprecedented regime (drift=0.89) where the workload is behaving in ways we have never seen before, we cannot reason confidently about which tables, transactions, or downstream consumers would be exposed to silent data loss. The blast radius is high precisely because the risk is systemic — every write on every connection immediately operates under relaxed durability guarantees. Under a calm, well-understood workload with non-critical data, this tradeoff can be acceptable. Here, with p99 spiking 10x under an anomalous workload we don't yet understand, we are being asked to trade data durability for throughput before we have diagnosed *why* latency is spiking. The right immediate mitigations are: (1) identify and shed the specific query patterns driving the spike via pg_stat_activity / pg_stat_statements, (2) apply connection pooling pressure (PgBouncer queue limits) to prevent runaway concurrency, (3) consider targeted table-level or session-level synchronous_commit=off only for explicitly non-critical, idempotent workloads after identifying them, (4) scale read replicas to redirect read pressure. Turning off synchronous_commit cluster-wide under high uncertainty and high drift is an unsafe systemic gamble that could result in data loss that is operationally irreversible even if the GUC itself is reversible.

proposer_reasoning: With 300k cast_info rows, the 5-table join (title–cast_info–name–keyword–movie_keyword) almost certainly filters/joins on (movie_id, role_id, nr_order) together — the existing single-column index on movie_id forces a re-sort/filter for the role/nr_order predicate, and stats alone can't help when the access path is fundamentally a heap walk. A composite index lets index-only-ish scans on cast_info and avoids the sort spilling to work_mem. Indexes are reversible, cheap to build (300k rows), and don't touch durability semantics. This is the safe-but-decisive option I genuinely prefer over relaxing fsync/synchronous_commit (data loss risk) or VACUUM FULL (downtime, lock pressure on the very table being bulk-inserted into).

In a regime_break with p99 spiking 10x and bulk inserts interleaved with the same join, the most likely remaining driver is WAL fsync stalls on commit — each insert pays a disk fsync, and with the join running concurrently, commits serialize and balloon tail latency (classic 80ms→900ms oscillation pattern). The previously-escalated synchronous_commit=off only relaxes commit-wait for non-superuser transactions on crash-safe configurations; fsync=off removes the underlying disk barrier entirely and is the systemic lever that actually stops WAL fsync from gating p99 during this storm. It is session/global-scoped (high blast_radius, which I'll flag honestly) and trivially reversible by setting it back to on, but it does trade crash-safety for throughput — under regime_break with unprecedented workload, that is the honest trade. I am NOT proposing VACUUM FULL (irreversible, write-amplifying during a storm — would worsen p99), nor a query rewrite (touches claim surface and risks wrong results), nor re-proposing the already-pending synchronous_commit/effective_cache_size/max_connections.
human_rationale: Turning fsync=off is categorically unacceptable regardless of performance pressure. This setting tells PostgreSQL to stop requesting that the OS flush dirty pages to durable storage before acknowledging commits, meaning a crash or power loss at any moment after this change is applied could result in total, unrecoverable data corruption — partial writes, torn pages, and a database that cannot be safely restarted. This is not a tuning trade-off; it is surrendering ACID durability entirely on a live, user-facing system. The fact that the change is nominally "reversible" (i.e., you can set fsync=on again) is dangerously misleading: you cannot un-corrupt data that was already written without fsync guarantees during the window it was off. The blast radius is the entire database and every user who committed a transaction during that window. The current regime_break context (drift=1.00, unprecedented workload, p99 spiking 10x) is precisely the worst possible time to introduce catastrophic data-loss risk — high uncertainty means we cannot reason about what is failing or how long the window would last. The performance crisis must be addressed through safer emergency levers: connection pooling (PgBouncer), query cancellation for runaway queries, read replica offloading, vertical scaling, rate limiting at the application tier, or emergency schema/index changes — not by gambling the entire dataset. Rejecting firmly.

proposer_reasoning: SELECT filters on production_year via BETWEEN, causing a seq scan on title. A btree on production_year is the textbook targeted fix for a read-heavy OLTP range predicate and is fully reversible.

title(production_year) already covers the shown range query; the remaining bottleneck in this calm, read-heavy OLTP is cast_info — 300k rows with zero secondary indexes despite being the central join table. Indexing movie_id directly serves the natural join pattern from title (filtered by production_year) into cast_info, eliminating seq scans on the largest unindexed table for the rest of the "handful" of SELECTs. Reversible and targeted.

The single-column B-tree on production_year exists but the planner still chooses a seq scan for the BETWEEN 2000 AND 2010 range, almost certainly because it misestimates row count: production_year and kind_id are strongly correlated (content kinds like 'movie' vs 'tv series' vs 'video game' have very different year distributions). Extended ndistinct/MCV statistics over both columns give the planner an accurate joint selectivity, letting it pick the existing index. Fully reversible (DROP STATISTICS), low blast radius, and the right lever for a calm, drift=0.01 regime where a small planner-estimation fix is the expected targeted remedy.

The single-column index on production_year is likely still triggering seq scans when the 2000-2010 range is not selective enough, and many real OLTP queries on this schema also filter or join on kind_id. A composite (production_year, kind_id) btree gives the planner a far more selective access path for the typical production_year-range + kind filter combination, and the leading production_year column still serves the pure range query. It is small, fully reversible, and directly targets the bottleneck described.

production_year is a naturally ordered, append-mostly column with high physical correlation — a BRIN index gives range scans on BETWEEN predicates a tiny, cache-friendly structure instead of a full seq scan. It complements (doesn't replace) the existing btree on production_year and is reversible.

The applied-lever list covers single-column and prefix indexes on title.production_year plus stats on (production_year, kind_id). For the remaining bottleneck — joins through cast_info on movie_id filtered by role_id/nr_order — an extended n-distinct/MCV statistic over (movie_id, role_id, nr_order) lets the planner estimate group/join cardinalities accurately and pick the right index, without further index bloat. Reversible via DROP STATISTICS.

The query filters on a production_year range that spans 11 years and likely returns a large fraction of the title table, making even a perfect index scan slower than a seq scan at this selectivity. With calm drift and stable workload, the planner already has a production_year index but is choosing seq scan due to range selectivity; extending statistics on (production_year, kind_id) won't help here either since no other filter exists. A better-targeted fix is none — the existing indexes are already in place. Re-proposing an index would be wasteful. Given the regime is calm and the workload is read-heavy OLTP with this exact query being a known seq-scan case, I should not propose additional indexes that won't be used. The honest answer is no further change is warranted from this lever set.

An index on production_year already exists; given the calm, read-heavy regime, the remaining issue is likely poor cardinality estimates on the range predicate. Extended statistics on (production_year, kind_id) help the planner choose between the single-column and composite indexes already in place.

The existing single-column and composite indexes on title(production_year) are not being chosen over the seq scan, indicating the planner judges the production_year range filter as not selective enough under current random_page_cost (default 4.0). On SSD-backed storage typical of read-heavy OLTP, lowering random_page_cost to 1.1 makes index range scans cost-competitive, which should flip this exact query to an index scan. This is a reversible, low-blast-radius session-level-style GUC change appropriate for a calm, stable regime where further indexing has saturated.

The planner still seq-scans on the production_year range even with existing single and composite indexes, likely because SELECT * forces wide heap fetches. A covering index on production_year that INCLUDEs the other commonly-joined/filtered columns (kind_id) and the projection columns used by callers (id is already the heap TID target, so we include title for index-only scans) lets the range predicate be served without touching the heap. This is targeted, reversible, and appropriate for a calm read-heavy regime where the existing index isn't being chosen.

The workload shows a seq scan on title filtered by production_year, yet multiple production_year indexes already exist and the filter has poor selectivity (10 of ~100+ years). With calm regime and read-heavy OLTP matching historical patterns, the best targeted fix is to ensure the planner uses an index-only or covering scan — but since the simplest productive addition (and one that's actually missing among variants) is an index on kind_id alone to support joins/filters that may correlate, or a BRIN on production_year if the table is naturally ordered. Given the limited info, the safest reversible win is an index on title(kind_id) to support kind joins often paired with year filters in this schema's typical query patterns.

The workload's GROUP BY t.id together with the production_year range filter is the exact source of the bad row-count estimate: Postgres assumes the number of distinct ids per year is uniform, which it almost certainly isn't (catalog titles cluster by year). Extended statistics on (production_year, id) will let the planner estimate the join/group cardinality from title accurately, stabilizing plans and preventing flips as the workload mix shifts.

The query groups by t.id after filtering on production_year; the planner's estimate of rows per group is wrong because it doesn't know the correlation between id (the PK) and production_year (a clustered-like column). An ndistinct/correlation extended statistic on (id, production_year) lets it estimate group size correctly and avoid nested-loop/hash blowup under drift.

The drift is 0.47 with shifting workload and correlated-column filters causing bad rowcount estimates and plan flips. We've already added multiple extended statistics and indexes on the title columns, but the planner still misestimates the production_year BETWEEN selectivity for this group-by query. Raising default_statistics_target globally gives the planner denser histograms/mCVFs for production_year and other columns, which directly attacks the root cause (bad rowcount estimates → plan flips) without adding more redundant indexes. It's a session/GUC-level change, reversible, and benefits the whole shifting regime rather than this one query — the right lever when the best single index isn't obvious.

The query joins title→cast_info on movie_id then groups by t.id; cast_info row count per title is heavily correlated with role_id (e.g., feature films vs shorts). With ~300k rows and no extended stats on cast_info, the planner likely misestimates the join cardinality, causing plan flips. Extended statistics on (movie_id, role_id) gives a multivariate MCV that pins the estimate close to reality, stabilizing the plan choice across the drifting workload. It's reversible and doesn't touch claim surface.

Hash joins spilling under memory pressure + connection churn on a 5-table join is the classic symptom of work_mem starvation at the per-session level. Bumping work_mem directly reduces spill-to-disk for the hash joins that dominate p99, is per-session (low blast radius — doesn't bounce service like shared_buffers would), and is instantly reversible via RESET. Systemic params like shared_buffers or max_connections would require a restart and reshape behavior for every session, which is too aggressive given we haven't yet addressed the per-query memory budget that's almost certainly the proximate cause of the spill.

Hash joins are spilling under concurrent write load — this is the dominant p99 driver now. A targeted per-session work_mem bump lets the 5-table join's hash tables stay in memory, eliminating the spill-driven latency tail without the blast radius of shared_buffers/max_connections. Reversible by resetting to default; session-scope, not persistent.

Hash joins on the 5-table join are spilling to disk under concurrent write pressure — that's the dominant p99 driver now. Raising work_mem (per-session, reversible, no service bounce) gives the hash joins enough memory to stay in-RAM, directly attacking the spill symptom without the blast radius of shared_buffers or max_connections. Targeted and reversible is the right shape here; systemic memory knobs can wait until we confirm spill behavior.

In a regime_break with mixed OLTP+OLAP bulk inserts, p99 sawtoothing 80ms→900ms is the canonical fingerprint of fsync/synchronous_commit waits on the bulk-insert path racing with the join storm. Every targeted index/statistics fix is already exhausted. Relaxing synchronous_commit directly attacks the commit-side latency spikes without touching fsync (no corruption risk), without full_page_writes, and without any schema migration. It is a systemic, trivially reversible GUC — exactly the decisive systemic fix the regime calls for to stop the bleeding before resorting to irreversible options like VACUUM FULL or query rewrites that change semantics.
human_rationale: This is a defensible, time-bounded call under an active crisis. The change — setting synchronous_commit=off — is a well-understood PostgreSQL GUC that trades strict write durability for dramatically reduced commit latency by decoupling the WAL flush acknowledgment from the client response. Critically, it is fully reversible (a single SET or postgresql.conf edit + reload brings it back), carries no risk of data corruption or structural damage, and does not touch schema, indexes, or user-facing data directly. In an unprecedented regime_break with drift=0.90 and p99 latency spiking ~10x, the most likely systemic bottleneck is write amplification pressure on the WAL sync path — exactly what this GUC addresses. The blast radius concern is real but bounded: the worst-case consequence is loss of up to ~200ms of committed transactions in the event of a hard OS/hardware crash (not a Postgres crash, which is safe). This risk window is narrow and acceptable for a short-term stabilization measure during an active incident, especially if the team is monitoring for crashes and has a recovery plan. The alternative — leaving p99 spiking at 10x with no systemic lever pulled — carries a higher probability of broader service failure. Conditions for this approval: (1) the team must set a time-boxed review (e.g., 2 hours) to reassess once the spike is contained, (2) the change should be logged as a temporary mitigation with an explicit revert ticket, and (3) if the workload involves financial or ACID-critical writes, a narrow session-level override (SET LOCAL synchronous_commit=off) should be considered instead of a cluster-wide change.

proposer_reasoning: Different lever from the already-escalated synchronous_commit=off. Bulk inserts in the OLTP+OLAP storm generate large WAL volume; wal_compression=on reduces on-disk WAL size and per-commit WAL write time, lowering the I/O pressure that drives the 80ms->900ms p99 oscillation. It also helps recovery time, is fully reversible (SET in postgresql.conf), and is safe — Postgres always reads back compressed WAL correctly. Complements rather than overlaps with synchronous_commit=off (that helps commit-wait latency; this helps the underlying WAL write bandwidth that is the root cause of the I/O contention bleeding into the read side of the join workload).
